_patch_meta.description: Dimension review patch — C03 (awe, contentment, delight, gratitude, joy, rejoicing, wonder, gladness, comfort): 122 dimension assignments, 7 group description corrections, 12 Session B findings, 5 Session D pointers

operations.description: Group 359-001: revised context_description (Phase B QA-VAGUE correction)

record.finding: Awe spans both Affective/Emotional and Spiritual/God-ward dimensions. God-ward groups (1682-001, 1682-003, 703-002, 703-003) show that the deepest fear/awe content is Spiritual/God-ward rather than merely affective. Session B should examine whether awe functions as a distinct inner-being category integrating affective intensity and God-ward orientation — the two are inseparable in the data.

Both contentment terms name a stable inner condition independent of circumstances — a learned/acquired disposition of sufficiency, not a transient emotional state. Session B should examine whether contentment constitutes a distinct sub-dimension of Character/Disposition, and how it relates to the Philippians 4 context where it appears alongside joy and peace.

The delight vocabulary spans every dimension of the inner being. This breadth suggests delight is not a single category but a family of engagements organised around the axis of what the inner person is oriented toward. Session B analysis should map this orientation axis as the structuring principle of the registry.

H2654A (cha.phets) and H2654B (cha.phats) produce parallel three-group structures with equivalent classifications. Their functional equivalence across the corpus is a lexical data point for Session B — whether the two forms represent a single semantic range or carry distinguishable nuances.

Group 3090-001 (hēdonē — pleasure as the competing inner orientation, desires that war within) occupies a significant counter-position to the positive delight vocabulary. Pleasure as the adversary of the will toward God is a distinctive NT inner-being concept. Session B should examine the hēdonē / delight tension as a structural feature of the inner life.

All three gratitude terms converge on Character/Disposition — gratitude is not a transient affective response but a stable inner reorientation of the whole person. The absence of gratitude as a diagnostic marker of inner corruption (5480-001) is a particularly significant finding. Session B should examine gratitude as a diagnostic indicator of inner-being health.

After dimension correction, 27 of 40 joy groups align with Spiritual/God-ward. Joy in Scripture is not primarily a free-floating affective state but is characteristically grounded in and directed toward God, his salvation, his presence, and his word. Session B should foreground this as the dominant finding of the registry.

Three groups name joy that persists through or is intensified by suffering: 364-001 (salad — defiant exultation), 381-002 (chairo — paradoxical joy in tribulation), and 3859-001 in Registry 42 (hēdista — most gladly in weakness). This convergent cross-term pattern names a distinct inner-being mode. Session B should examine joy-through-suffering as a structural feature rather than an anomaly.

Group 378-001 (chara — abiding inner state) is assigned Character/Disposition rather than Affective/Emotional because it names a stable characterising inner quality. This parallels gratitude in Registry 69. Both registries produce at least one group where the inner state is stable and dispositional rather than episodic. Session B should examine whether there is a category of joy-as-character-quality distinct from joy-as-affective-experience.

Wonder vocabulary divides between the divine side (Theological/Divine-Human) and the human response (Affective/Emotional). Session B should examine wonder as the inner state at the interface between human capacity and divine transcendence — the point where the inner person's capacity to comprehend is exceeded by what God is and does.

Ya.tav (H3190) produces three materially distinct dimension patterns: Affective/Emotional (merry heart), Moral/Conscience (moral goodness), and Affective/Emotional (approval/pleasure). This bridges the moral and affective dimensions within a single gladness/goodness term. Session B should examine whether the Hebrew gladness/goodness vocabulary reveals an integration of moral and affective categories that the programme's dimension vocabulary treats separately.

Comfort vocabulary splits cleanly: God-given comfort (Spiritual/God-ward) and community-given comfort (Relational/Social). Paraklēsis spans both dimensions across its three groups. Session B should examine the structural relationship between divine and human comfort — the data suggests divine comfort both enables and models the human comfort that flows from it.

record.description: The commanded-inner-state pattern — Scripture commanding specific inner orientations (do not be dismayed / fear the Lord / rejoice always) — appears across Registries 11, 97, 42 and likely others. Session D should trace this pattern across clusters and examine its implications for understanding the inner being as subject to divine instruction.

The Theological/Divine-Human cluster within delight/joy registries — God's pleasure/displeasure as the criterion of acceptance — names a structural feature of the inner-being relationship with God: divine inner states (delight, pleasure, exultation) are constitutive of the human inner-being condition. Session D should examine divine inner states as a programme-level category and their relationship to human inner states across all clusters.

Morally negative joy/gladness appears across multiple C03 registries: 355-002 and 365-002 (R97), 1096-001 (R132), 634-002 (R186). The same inner-being faculty appears in both ordered and disordered forms. Session D should examine how the positive/negative pairing of inner states recurs across the programme — whether positive inner capacities have systematic corrupted counterparts.

Two groups name the inner life as designed toward an end: 378-002 (chara — joy as the goal/completion of the inner life in God) and 6701-001 (miph.la.ah — wondrous works designed to evoke wonder). Researcher note: this may also include what God intends as the inner state of the person. These groups point toward a dimension the existing vocabulary does not name — the inner life as oriented toward a divinely intended goal. Session D should examine whether a new vocabulary category is needed.

Group 3090-001 (hēdonē) occupies a counter-position to the positive delight vocabulary. The pleasure/delight tension in the NT — where hēdonē names the adversary of the ordered inner life — is a significant cross-registry pattern. Session D should examine the structured opposition between competing inner orientations as a programme-level finding.
